:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

In Idaho, a felon is anyone sentenced to a crime punishable by death or imprisonment in the state prison. Idaho felons must be sentenced by a judge to a minimum term of incarceration with no parole eligibility. The judge may then impose an additional indeterminate sentence, during which inmates may be released on parole. These two portions of the sentence, often called a “unified sentence” may not exceed the statutory maximum set for the crime. Correctional employees make a sentencing calculation when an inmate is committed to the Department of Correction. Based on this information, the Commission conducts a review to determine the initial parole hearing date, which is set at most six months before an inmate’s first parole eligibility (i.e., six months before the minimum term expires). All inmates who are eligible for parole release must be considered at least once by the Commission.


People who were Eligible for Parole

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison, accounting for 86% (2,255 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.



In 2020, there were 520 people who were incarcerated for a parole revocation and were eligible for parole but not yet released from prison.

``` ::: :::
::: {.cell} :::
## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population

In 2020, there were 3,129 people who were eligible for parole but not released from prison, constituting 38% of the parole-eligible prison population.




``` ::: :::

## Offenses ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 2,221 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 71% of the parole-eligible prison population.
